my_sentence: Most of my coworkers will have a rest, so we are rest.
correction: Most of my coworkers will rest, so we'll rest.
explanation: "Rest" is the verb here, so it can't follow "are" as if it were a noun/adjective. Use the verb directly: "we'll rest" (or "we'll take a rest"). "We are rest" is not a valid structure.
correct_examples: My coworkers will rest tomorrow.
We'll take a break this afternoon.
context: Talking about a Japanese holiday and most coworkers resting

my_sentence: Many people need to find a job to consume their energy.
correction: Many people need to find jobs to consume their energy.
explanation: When making a general statement about people in general, use the bare plural ("jobs"), not the singular "a job". The plural matches the plural subject "many people" and expresses the generic idea.
correct_examples: People look for jobs that match their skills.
Students need part-time jobs to earn money.
context: Writing about the importance / pros of jobs

my_sentence: They can use their abilities to provide services and make some contribution for the society.
correction: They can use their abilities to provide services and contribute to society.
explanation: Two issues: use the verb "contribute" rather than "make a contribution" here, and the collocation is "contribute TO" (not "for"). Also "society" as a general concept usually takes no article.
correct_examples: Volunteers contribute to society in many ways.
Everyone can contribute to the community.
context: Explaining how jobs benefit society

my_sentence: It's very useful to help society to progress.
correction: It can be useful to help society progress.
explanation: After "help + object", use the bare infinitive (no "to"): "help society progress", not "help society to progress".
correct_examples: This tool helps students learn faster.
The new policy helped the company grow.
context: Explaining how jobs help society develop

my_sentence: I ordered a delivery to send some fruits to my another apartment.
correction: I ordered a delivery to send some fruit to my other apartment. / to another apartment.
explanation: "Another" cannot follow a possessive like "my". Say either "my other apartment" or "another apartment", not "my another apartment".
correct_examples: I left my keys in the other room.
Let's move to another table.
context: Ordering a delivery to take fruit to a second apartment

my_sentence: I don't have time to send by myself, so I ask someone for help.
correction: I don't have time to send it myself, so I asked someone for help.
explanation: A transitive verb like "send" needs an object: "send it". "Send by myself" drops the thing being sent. "Myself" alone (without "by") is enough to mean 'on my own'.
correct_examples: I can deliver it myself.
She fixed it herself.
context: Why he used a delivery service instead of going himself

my_sentence: This can reduce several criminal events.
correction: This can reduce several criminal activities.
explanation: Use "criminal activities", not "criminal events". "Events" are things that happen/occur; "activities" are the actions or doings people carry out, which is what crime refers to.
correct_examples: The police cracked down on criminal activities.
These programs reduce gang activity in the area.
context: Reading his written sentences about jobs reducing crime

my_sentence: I ordered a delivery. Its delivery is so quick, just 10 minutes.
correction: I ordered a delivery. It was so quick, just 10 minutes.
explanation: Since the delivery already happened (past event), describe it in the past tense: "it was so quick", not the present "is".
correct_examples: The food arrived fast; it was so quick.
The trip was great, only two hours.
context: Describing how fast the delivery service was

my_sentence: I haven't experienced it, but many people had have experienced it.
correction: I haven't experienced it, but many people have. / but many people did.
explanation: Don't stack auxiliaries ("had have experienced"). After the contrast, just use the bare auxiliary instead of repeating the whole verb: "...but many people have" (or "...but many people did").
correct_examples: I haven't seen it, but my friends have.
He didn't finish, but the others did.
context: Talking about the eye-twitch reflex

my_sentence: You can use Grok to search information very quickly and convenient.
correction: You can use Grok to search for information very quickly and conveniently.
explanation: Modifying a verb ("search") requires an adverb, so coordinate two adverbs: "quickly and conveniently", not the adjective "convenient".
correct_examples: The app works quickly and smoothly.
She explained it clearly and patiently.
context: Comparing AI tools like Grok

my_sentence: You will find a lot of problem in it, and for some question it can't understand.
correction: You will find a lot of problems in it, and there are some questions it can't understand.
explanation: Countable nouns after "a lot of" and "some" must be plural: "a lot of problems", "some questions".
correct_examples: There are a lot of bugs in this code.
I have some questions about the plan.
context: Saying Grok's code output has many problems

my_sentence: Many years ago I used it, but now I didn't use it.
correction: Many years ago I used it, but now I'm not using it anymore.
explanation: For a current situation, use the present ("I'm not using it anymore" / "I don't use it anymore"), not the past "I didn't use it". Past tense would refer to a finished past time, which contradicts "now".
correct_examples: I used to play tennis, but now I don't.
I had a car before, but I'm not driving anymore.
context: Talking about no longer using Netflix

my_sentence: That's because the Japan company, they don't trust.
correction: That's because Japanese companies don't trust them.
explanation: Use the adjective form "Japanese" to describe companies, not the country noun "Japan": "Japanese companies". Also avoid the redundant double subject ("the company, they...") — just "Japanese companies don't trust...".
correct_examples: Japanese companies value teamwork.
Many Chinese companies export to Europe.
context: Why his company uses Google/Anthropic models instead of Chinese ones

my_sentence: 20 years ago in my country, there are many people lose their job.
correction: 20 years ago in my country, there were many people who lost their jobs.
explanation: A past time marker ("20 years ago") needs past tense: "there were" and "lost". Also, to attach the action to "people", use a relative clause: "people who lost their jobs", and the plural "jobs".
correct_examples: There were many workers who lost their jobs during the crisis.
Years ago, many people moved to the cities.
context: Example of unemployment leading to crime

my_sentence: If someone finish his or her work, he or she will be very tired.
correction: If someone finishes his or her work, he or she will be very tired.
explanation: Third-person singular subjects ("someone", "he", "she") take the -s form of the verb in the present: "finishes".
correct_examples: When he finishes work, he goes home.
If someone needs help, she calls me.
context: Saying busy people are too tired to commit crimes

my_sentence: They will feel so tired, tiring and exhausting.
correction: They will feel so tired and exhausted.
explanation: Use the -ed participle for how a person feels ("tired", "exhausted"); the -ing form ("tiring", "exhausting") describes what causes the feeling. A person is exhausted; the work is exhausting.
correct_examples: After the trip I was exhausted.
The kids were tired after playing all day.
context: Describing how people feel after finishing work

my_sentence: If you did a bad thing, you will be catch quickly.
correction: If you do a bad thing, you will be caught quickly.
explanation: The passive needs the past participle: "be caught", not the base form "catch". ("Catch-caught-caught".)
correct_examples: Criminals are usually caught on camera.
He was caught cheating on the test.
context: Saying CCTV cameras mean criminals get caught fast

my_sentence: If you do bad thing, you will be caught.
correction: If you do a bad thing, you will be caught.
explanation: A singular countable noun like "thing" needs an article: "a bad thing".
correct_examples: If you make a mistake, fix it quickly.
He did a good deed today.
context: Talking about CCTV deterring crime

my_sentence: For an apartment, maybe hundreds of cameras in it.
correction: For an apartment, there would be hundreds of cameras in it.
explanation: An English clause needs a subject and verb. To state that something exists, use "there is / there are / there would be": "there would be hundreds of cameras", not just "hundreds of cameras in it".
correct_examples: There are many shops in this mall.
There would be a lot of people at the event.
context: Estimating how many CCTV cameras an apartment complex has

my_sentence: That's because there are too many CCTV camera company.
correction: That's because there are too many CCTV camera companies.
explanation: After "too many", the countable noun must be plural: "companies".
correct_examples: There are too many cars on the road.
She has too many books to read.
context: Explaining why CCTV cameras are cheap in China
